channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Wants to Call the Shots. But in Iran, He Keeps Hitting His Limits.
item.description: President Trump is grappling with his own version of the sort of Middle East crisis that beset his predecessors, and that he promised to avoid.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump insists that he has not violated his campaign pledge to avoid “endless wars,” even though a conflict he called “a little excursion” in March has entered its fourth month.

item.title: The 15-Hour Fight With Iran Showed the Bind Israel Is In
item.description: As it braces for a possible U.S.-Iranian peace deal, Israel now knows that if it responds forcefully to attacks by Hezbollah, Iran may strike Israel with missiles.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Ronen Zvulun
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel in Jerusalem in March.

item.title: Iran and Israel Pull Back
item.description: Also, the N.B.A. finals arrive in New York. Here’s the latest at the end of Monday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tehran on Monday.

item.title: Trump Nominates Blanche for Attorney General, Setting Up Confirmation Fight
item.description: As acting attorney general, Todd Blanche has shown a willingness to execute the president’s maximalist demands. Whether the Senate will confirm him remains unclear.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Glenn Thrush
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche’s role in the attempted creation of President Trump’s $1.8 billion payout fund gave rise to tensions with Senate Republicans, who will now have to decide whether to confirm him.

item.title: How the Drive to Find a Conspiracy Against Trump Rocked the Justice Dept.
item.description: The push to investigate what the president’s allies saw as a “deep state” cabal intent on taking him down set off cascading crises, ended careers and undercut the department’s credibility with judges.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The F.B.I. director Kash Patel’s “grand conspiracy case” sought to tie together actions by a group of people that President Trump blamed for various investigations into him.

item.title: Pentagon Again Revises Religious Categories for Troops
item.description: The Defense Department made the change after lawmakers objected to its original list, which did not include the Latter-day Saints among traditions labeled Christian.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Mormons (Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Mike Lee of Utah objected to a Pentagon policy memo excluding Mormons from being categorized as Christian.

item.title: Trump Visit Closes Blocks Around MSG: What to Know About Security at the Knicks Game
item.description: With President Trump and Mayor Zohran Mamdani set to attend Monday night’s Knicks game at Madison Square Garden, there will be strict security in Midtown Manhattan.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Southall
item.category: Moynihan Train Hall at Pennsylvania Station (Manhattan, NY)
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban for The New York Times
item.media:description: There will be airport-style security at checkpoints in the blocks surrounding Madison Square Garden on Monday night.

item.title: A Timeline of Donald Trump and Lindsey Graham’s Relationship Before the South Carolina Primary
item.description: Once one of President Trump’s fiercest critics, the South Carolina senator has dramatically shifted his posture over the years. Will it pay off on Tuesday in his primary?
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner and Meg Felling
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times, Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Lindsey Graham of South Carolina, left, and President Trump.

item.title: What’s Taking So Long to Count California Ballots?
item.description: Other states with vote-by-mail manage to count much more quickly.
item.dc:creator: Maia Spoto
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Election workers processing ballots for the California state primary at the Los Angeles County Ballot Processing Center on Friday in City of Industry, Calif.

item.title: Ken Paxton’s Former Defense Lawyer Endorses James Talarico
item.description: The lawyer, Dan Cogdell, helped save Mr. Paxton from criminal charges and an impeachment, but now he says the Texas attorney general has “lost sight of his mission.”
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dan Cogdell, a defense attorney for Ken Paxton, at the State Capitol in Austin during Mr. Paxton’s impeachment trial in 2023.

item.title: Best and Worst Moments From the 2026 Tony Awards: Pink, Maya Rudolph, Cole Escola, ‘Cats’
item.description: Pink brought sincerity and high energy as the host, musical numbers by “Cats,” “Ragtime” and “Chicago” popped, and Maya Rudolph and Cole Escola mined comic gold.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Pink, center, flanked by Megan Thee Stallion, Neil Patrick Harris and other performers during the opening number at the Tony Awards.

item.title: Unforgettable Looks at the Tony Awards: Cole Escola, Sarah Paulson and More
item.description: Shrunken suits, bouncy wigs and gloves that came with a manicure were some of the looks that stood out on the red carpet.
item.dc:creator: The Styles Desk
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cole Escola in Christopher John Rogers at the 79th Tony Awards.

item.title: On Broadway’s Biggest Night, ‘Saturday Night Live’ Shows Its Strength
item.description: The long-running sketch comedy show proved its power during the Tony Awards on Sunday, with Lorne Michaels and several former stars grabbing the spotlight.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor and Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Lorne Michaels, the creator of “Saturday Night Live,” accepting the Tony Award for best new musical on Sunday.

item.title: Judge Throws Out Trump Administration’s Policy Imposing $100,000 Fees for H-1B Visas
item.description: The ruling voided “in its entirety” a policy from September requiring companies to pay $100,000 fees for H-1B visa petitions.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Brian Snyder/Reuters
item.media:description: Judge Leo T. Sorokin wrote that the Trump administration’s policy appeared to step on Congress’s “exclusive power” to levy taxes.

item.title: U.S. Denies Entry to World Cup Referee From Somalia
item.description: Omar Artan was one of 52 referees selected for the FIFA World Cup. But on Saturday he was denied entry because of “vetting concerns,” U.S. officials said.
item.dc:creator: Hussein Mohamed and Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Khaled Desouki/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Omar Artan will not officiate games at the World Cup, FIFA said.

item.title: U.S. Judge Reverses Decision on Colombian Woman Deported to Congo
item.description: The judge had previously ruled that the woman had been improperly deported by the Trump administration and had ordered her returned to the United States.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.media:credit: Justin Makangara for The New York Times
item.media:description: A hotel in Kinshasa, Democratic Republic of Congo, housing deportees from the United States in April.

item.title: Trump Administration Seeks to Revoke U.S. Citizenship of 17 Immigrants
item.description: The push to denaturalize more immigrants is the latest sign that the administration is setting its sights on the legal immigration system.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: “We will continue to use every lawful avenue to denaturalize and remove aliens,” Markwayne Mullin, the homeland security secretary, said in a statement.

item.title: They Spent Years on a Math Problem. Then They Were Scooped by A.I.
item.description: Artificial intelligence is mastering the kinds of projects that have long helped to build the careers of young mathematicians. What does that mean for their future?
item.dc:creator: Gregory Barber

item.title: OpenAI Files Confidentially for IPO as AI Companies Rush to Wall St.
item.description: The company hopes to raise billions in a highly anticipated public offering that could unlock a new generation of tech industry wealth.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: OpenAI’s ChatGPT chatbot started the artificial intelligence boom.

item.title: Apple Reveals New A.I.-Powered Version of Its Siri Digital Assistant
item.description: The iPhone maker revealed its new artificial intelligence products at its developer conference, the last with Tim Cook as chief executive.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Apple held its annual conference for software developers on Monday in Cupertino, Calif.

item.title: Shootout in Olde Town Square: A Cop, a Killer and a Good Guy With a Gun
item.description: No community was better prepared for active shooters than Arvada, Colo. Then men with guns converged in the city center, each on different missions.
item.dc:creator: Mike Baker
item.media:credit: Helen H. Richardson/The Denver Post, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Police at the scene of a shooting in Arvada, Colo., in June 2021.

item.title: GLP-1 Drugs: 6 Things We’ve Learned About Their Effects
item.description: The widespread use of drugs like Ozempic is giving scientists a clearer picture than ever of their effects.
item.dc:creator: Dani Blum

item.title: How Elon Musk’s Friendship With the F.C.C. Smooths the Way for SpaceX’s I.P.O.
item.description: Brendan Carr, the chairman of the Federal Communications Commission, has greenlighted regulatory requests for the company’s Starlink satellite internet service and lavished praise on its chief executive.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brendan Carr, chairman of the Federal Communications Commission.

item.title: How Banks Are Using the SpaceX IPO to Woo the Super Rich
item.description: Wall Street is giving its richest clients velvet rope access to this week’s public listing of SpaceX, reflecting the growing importance of the wealth management business.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Callaghan O'Hare for The New York Times
item.media:description: SpaceX’s I.P.O. will be an opportunity for banks to nurture their relationships with their richest clients and develop new ones, too.

item.title: Vance Blames Migrant ‘Invasion’ for UK Stabbing
item.description: British officials accused Vice President JD Vance of trying to “stir up division” in his comments on the murder of Henry Nowak, whose killer was sentenced to life in prison last week.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:credit: Ben Stansall/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Tributes for Henry Nowak outside the Portswood Police Station in Southampton, southern England, last week.

item.title: A ‘Miraculous Transformation’: How Kim Jong-un Fortified North Korea
item.description: He used the pandemic to ruthlessly tighten his grip on the country. Then he energized its economy by leveraging Russia’s war in Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Athit Perawongmetha/Reuters
item.media:description: Kim Jong-un arriving at the Dong Dang railway station in Vietnam, near the Chinese border, in 2019.

item.title: ‘Backrooms’ and ‘Obsession’: The Lessons Behind These Horror Hits
item.description: Two Times critics unpack the twin phenomenon of “Backrooms” and “Obsession” and what lessons should — and shouldn’t — be learned from their massive success.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson and Jason Zinoman

item.title: Why Everyone Wants Jon Ossoff to Run for President
item.description: The Georgia senator is excoriating Trump and his systemic corruption in a way that transcends the Democratic Party’s progressive-moderate divide.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg
item.media:credit: José Ibarra Rizo for The New York Times

item.title: The SpaceX I.P.O. Shows Us What’s Wrong With Our Stock Market
item.description: The SpaceX I.P.O. proves the stock market has become a hype machine.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Zamost

item.title: Hoyle Schweitzer, Who Brought Windsurfing to the Masses, Dies at 93
item.description: With his friend Jim Drake, he built the Windsurfer, a sailboard that was cheaper and more portable than most sailboats. It became a global phenomenon.
item.dc:creator: Daniel E. Slotnik
